summary: PR #388 deliberately replaces per-method/finality failsafe defaulting with 'user policies fully override defaults', then routes unmatched requests to a no-policy default executor at the tail of the executor slice. Combined with validateMatchers auto-prepending {Method:'*', Action:exclude} when users only define includes, this silently drops retry/timeout/hedge/circuit-breaker on any request that doesn't match the user's matchers. Span attributes still come from legacy fe.method/fe.finalities, so observability into which matcher actually fired is lost. matchers.Match also reads jrpcReq.Params without holding the request's RLock, which is racy under hedged execution where NormalizeHttpJsonRpc may concurrently mutate Params per-upstream. SetDefaults() in NewCachePolicy is properly idempotent. Recommended: deploy via shadow first, audit configs for partial-overlap (catch-all + per-method override) patterns, add a span/metric for matcher identity, and add an RLock around Params reads in matchers.Match.

findings.title: Unmatched methods silently fall through to no-policy default executor (no retry, timeout, hedge, or circuit breaker)
findings.description: When a user defines a single failsafe entry with action:include scoped to a subset of methods (e.g. `failsafe: [{matchers: [{method: eth_*, action: include}], retry: {maxAttempts: 5}, timeout: 30s}]`), validateMatchers auto-prepends `{Method: '*', Action: exclude}` (validation.go:54-61). matchers.Match() iterates last-to-first: for `trace_call` the eth_* include doesn't match, the catch-all exclude does, so Match returns false. getFailsafeExecutor then falls through to `failsafeExecutors[len-1]`, which networks_registry.go:148-156 constructs as `{config: nil, executor: failsafe.NewExecutor[*common.NormalizedResponse](), timeout: nil}` — a literally empty failsafe with no retry, no timeout, no hedge, and no circuit breaker. Result: trace_call requests run unprotected. This contradicts the natural reading of the config ('I added retry to my failsafe for eth_*; everything else gets default behavior'). Pre-PR, the per-method merge in SetDefaults would have applied the catch-all defaults from `NetworkDefaults.Failsafe[{matchMethod:'*', retry:5, timeout:120s, hedge:...}]` to all unmatched methods.
findings.impact: Methods not covered by user matchers get zero failsafe protection. A slow or hung upstream will hang requests indefinitely (no timeout). A transient upstream error returns immediately to the client (no retry). No circuit breaker means a flapping upstream keeps receiving traffic. This is the worst kind of reliability regression: silent, dependent on traffic mix, won't show in unit tests or staging unless the test config includes the same partial-include pattern.
findings.recommendation: Either (a) at config-load time, when user defines failsafe[] with partial coverage, log a warning and explicitly inherit the relevant default policies into the user-defined entries' unset fields, OR (b) refuse to start when validateMatchers auto-prepends `{*, exclude}` AND the resulting tail executor is the no-policy default — force the operator to explicitly add a catch-all failsafe entry. At minimum: (1) emit a Prometheus counter `erpc_failsafe_default_executor_used_total{network,method,scope}` and a WARN log on each fallthrough so operators can detect this in staging/shadow, and (2) document this behavior change with a `BREAKING` callout in the release notes plus a config-migration recipe.

findings.title: Per-method/finality default merging is removed; user policies fully override defaults — silent loss of retry/hedge inheritance
findings.description: Both `UpstreamConfig.SetDefaults` and `NetworkConfig.SetDefaults` now branch on `len(.Failsafe) > 0`: when the user defined ANY failsafe entry, only `fs.SetDefaults(nil)` runs (no inheritance from `defaults.Failsafe`). The branch comment explicitly says 'matchers PR (#388) deliberately replaced per-method/finality merging with user policies override defaults entirely'. Consequence: an existing config like `defaults.failsafe: [{matchMethod:'*', retry:{maxAttempts:3}, timeout:5s}]` plus `networks.failsafe: [{matchMethod:'trace_*', timeout:10s}]` previously merged retry=3 into the trace_* entry. Post-PR, the trace_* entry has no retry policy at all, and (combined with rel-001) any non-trace_* method silently goes to the no-policy default executor. This is documented as deliberate but is a config-shape semantics change that does not produce any error, warning, or metric on startup.
findings.impact: Depending on traffic mix, percent of requests with NO retry can jump from 0% to 100% on deploy. Trace methods are typically used for indexers and analytics — their loss of retry will manifest as elevated 5xx and is recoverable on retry by the client, but raw RPC clients that don't retry (most do not) will see degraded UX. Compounds with rel-001.
findings.recommendation: (1) Mandatory: include a config-validator pre-check that diffs the resolved per-method/finality failsafe matrix between pre-PR and post-PR semantics for the user's submitted config and prints a table of methods/finalities whose effective retry/timeout/hedge changed. (2) Roll out behind a feature flag (e.g. `EXPERIMENTAL_FAILSAFE_MATCHERS=true`) for at least one release so operators can opt in. (3) Goldsky shadow deploy is necessary but not sufficient — the goldsky shadow won't observe trace_* if their workload doesn't include traces. Need a config-time diff tool, not just runtime observation.

findings.title: matchers.Match reads jrpcReq.Params without acquiring the request's RLock
findings.description: matchers.Match (matcher.go:163-167) calls `req.JsonRpcRequest()` and dereferences `jrpcReq.Params` without holding the JsonRpcRequest's RLock. JsonRpcRequest exposes RLock/Lock — production code paths that read Params reliably acquire RLock first (see erpc/networks.go:1550, erpc/networks_static_responses.go:27, erpc/networks_interpolation_test.go:684), and `evm.NormalizeHttpJsonRpc` mutates Params under a write lock. Network.getFailsafeExecutor is called once per Forward (so single-threaded for that scope), but Upstream.getFailsafeExecutor (upstream.go:293, 604) is called inside the per-attempt loop that runs concurrently across hedge goroutines. Different upstreams have different latest-block heuristics, so per-upstream NormalizeHttpJsonRpc can interpolate `latest`/`finalized` tags into different concrete hex strings — i.e. it does mutate Params per attempt. Concurrent Match() reads of Params during NormalizeHttpJsonRpc writes is a classic data race that the Go race detector will flag and that may corrupt the `[]interface{}` slice header under load.
findings.impact: Likely benign for the common case where Params is a flat array of strings/bools/numbers (the slice header copy is cheap and a torn read just produces wrong matcher decisions for a single request). Worse case: panic on slice-of-interface{} type assertion against a torn header, or matchParam recursive map traversal observing a half-written map. The hottest path (configs without param matchers) reads Params but doesn't recurse into it, so the blast radius is limited unless users add Params-based matchers. This is exactly the configuration the PR enables, so adoption increases the risk surface.
findings.recommendation: Wrap the Params read in matchers.Match with `jrpcReq.RLock(); params := jrpcReq.Params; jrpcReq.RUnlock()` (matching the existing locking convention everywhere else). Add a `go test -race ./matchers/... ./upstream/... ./erpc/...` step to CI that exercises the hedge path with params-based matchers.

findings.title: Failsafe span attributes lose granularity; existing dashboards/alerts will see all-`*` matched_method
findings.description: Forward sets `failsafe.matched_method` and `failsafe.matched_finalities` from `failsafeExecutor.method` / `.finalities`, which networks_registry.go:125-138 derives from `fsCfg.MatchMethod` / `fsCfg.MatchFinality` — the legacy fields. For configs using only the new Matchers schema, MatchMethod is empty (defaulted to '*') and MatchFinality is nil. Every span will therefore show `failsafe.matched_method=*`, `failsafe.matched_finalities=[]`, regardless of which matcher actually selected the executor. Dashboards/alerts that filter or group by these attributes (e.g. SLO panels per method-class, alerts on retry-exhaustion for specific methods) will lose granularity post-deploy.
findings.impact: Breaks observability continuity. Operators won't be able to differentiate failsafe behavior per matcher in traces, and per-method failsafe SLOs become impossible to track without a config-side reverse-lookup. This is the kind of regression that's invisible until an incident, when on-call discovers their dashboards are now uniformly `*`.
findings.recommendation: (1) In NewNetwork/NewUpstream, compute and store a stable matcher fingerprint (e.g. `idx + first_matcher_method + first_finality`) alongside the executor. (2) Add a span attribute `failsafe.executor_index` (int) and `failsafe.matcher_summary` (string) on Forward, derived from the actual matched FailsafeExecutor. (3) Document a migration mapping for any dashboard/alert that filters by `failsafe.matched_method` so operators can re-tune before deploy. (4) Add an integration test that asserts span attributes are set per matcher index for a multi-matcher config.

findings.title: Default no-policy executor fallback is silent — config typos in matchers result in zero failsafe with no log or metric
findings.description: Both Network.getFailsafeExecutor (networks.go:286-291) and Upstream.getFailsafeExecutor (upstream.go:325-329) end with `if len(failsafeExecutors) > 0 { return failsafeExecutors[len-1] }`. The tail is always the no-policy default executor (config:nil, no timeout, no retry, no hedge). When a user's matchers fail to cover a request — whether by intent (rel-001), by typo (`method: eth_get_logs` missing the camelCase), or by Match returning false due to params mismatch — the fallback is taken silently. There is no debug log differentiating 'matched user executor[i]' from 'fell through to default no-policy executor'. The Network.getFailsafeExecutor does log the matched case (`Msg('matched failsafe executor')` at line 263-268) but does NOT log the fallthrough case. Operators have no signal that their failsafe is not applying.
findings.impact: A typo in a matcher's method pattern or a forgotten matcher entry results in production traffic running unprotected, with the only signal being downstream latency tails or 5xx spikes. This is hard to attribute to config drift.
findings.recommendation: (1) When `getFailsafeExecutor` returns the tail no-policy default, emit a WARN log (rate-limited) and increment a Prometheus counter `erpc_failsafe_unmatched_total{scope, network, method, finality}`. (2) Make the fallback configurable: add a `failsafe.requireExplicitMatch: true` knob that returns an error (and a 503 to the caller) instead of falling through silently. (3) Add a startup-time validator that warns when `len(failsafeExecutors) > 1` AND the only path to the tail no-policy executor for known methods (eth_call, eth_getLogs, etc.) is via auto-prepended `{*, exclude}` — this exact pattern from rel-001.

findings.title: Circuit breaker state is reset on every config reload (pre-existing, but matchers reload makes it more frequent)
findings.description: Each FailsafeExecutor wraps `failsafe.NewExecutor(policyArray...)` which constructs a fresh circuit-breaker policy with zeroed counters. Any config-reload path that reconstructs the Network/Upstream (which is the standard erpc reload flow) zeroes circuit-breaker state. This is pre-existing — not introduced by this PR — but the PR's matcher-based selection means reloads driven by adding/changing a single matcher entry will reset CB state for ALL methods of that scope, including ones whose underlying retry/timeout did not change. Operators relying on long HalfOpenAfter (default 5min) will see a flapping upstream re-enter the rotation immediately after every config reload.
findings.impact: Limited if config reloads are rare. A frequent-reload deployment pipeline (e.g. weekly canary rotations) will repeatedly re-flap traffic to known-bad upstreams.
findings.recommendation: Either (a) document this clearly in the CHANGELOG with the matchers PR rollout notes, or (b) longer-term, key circuit-breaker state by an upstream-stable identity (upstream id × method-class × finality) and persist across executor reconstructions. Out of scope for this PR but worth tracking as a follow-up.

findings.title: PolicyWithMatcher.EmptyState() will NPE if Matcher is nil; invariant is implicit
findings.description: PolicyWithMatcher.EmptyState (cache_policy.go:28) does `return pm.Matcher.Empty` with no nil check. The current contract is that findGetPolicies/findSetPolicies only construct PolicyWithMatcher when MatchesForGetWithMatcher / MatchesForSetWithMatcher returned a non-nil matcher (cache_policy.go:104-121, 128-204). However, EvmJsonRpcCache.Get (json_rpc_cache.go:281-285) reuses the `policyWithMatcher` loop variable post-loop and assigns it from `matchedPolicyWithMatcher` if available — both sources should have non-nil Matcher, but this invariant is not enforced or documented. A future caller constructing a PolicyWithMatcher with `Matcher: nil` (e.g. a test helper or admin debug endpoint) would NPE deep inside the cache hit path.
findings.impact: Currently safe given the call graph, but a single defensive nil-check is cheap insurance against a fragile invariant.
findings.recommendation: Add `if pm == nil || pm.Matcher == nil { return common.CacheEmptyBehaviorIgnore }` in PolicyWithMatcher.EmptyState, OR change the type to embed Matcher by value (eliminate the nil case). Add a godoc comment on PolicyWithMatcher stating the Matcher must be non-nil.

residual_risks.title: Goldsky shadow deploy is necessary but not sufficient
residual_risks.description: The PR author plans to shadow this on goldsky first, which is good for catching latency/error-rate regressions for goldsky's traffic mix. But the silent retry/timeout loss (rel-001, rel-002) only manifests for methods NOT covered by user matchers. A goldsky shadow won't surface regressions for trace_* methods if goldsky doesn't issue traces. Recommend: also enumerate the post-PR effective failsafe matrix per method-class for every production config and diff against pre-PR before deploy.

residual_risks.title: Existing dashboards and alerts must be re-tuned before deploy
residual_risks.description: Per rel-004, span attributes and (potentially) Prometheus labels lose granularity. Operators who alert on `failsafe.matched_method=trace_*` retries-exhausted will see those alerts go silent, not because the underlying issue is gone but because the label is now '*'. This is an alert-on-deploy hazard.

residual_risks.title: Config reload semantics for in-flight requests not addressed
residual_risks.description: When config is reloaded at runtime with new matchers, in-flight requests are still using the executor selected from the pre-reload matchers (since getFailsafeExecutor was called once at the top of Forward). This is the existing behavior and likely correct (we don't want a request to switch executors mid-flight), but the PR introduces matcher complexity that makes this implicit guarantee more important to reason about. Worth a comment in Forward stating that the selected executor is sticky for the request lifecycle.

residual_risks.title: validateMatchers mutates the matchers slice in place
residual_risks.description: validateMatchers prepends a catch-all `{*, exclude}` (or `{*, include}`) when only one action type is present (validation.go:54-69). This mutates the user-provided slice. Idempotent on repeated calls (verified — the prepended catch-all itself becomes part of the mixed-action set on second pass). However, if config is consumed by other parts of the system (admin UI, config diff/audit, exported metrics labels), the displayed matchers will include the auto-prepended rule, which may surprise operators. Cosmetic but worth documenting.

testing_gaps.title: No regression test asserting the no-policy fallback for unmatched methods
testing_gaps.description: There is no test that constructs `failsafe: [{matchers: [{method: eth_*, action: include}], retry:5}]` and asserts that a `trace_call` request runs WITHOUT retry/timeout (or, after a fix, WITH a sensible default). Add a network-level integration test under erpc/networks_test.go (or a new erpc/networks_matchers_fallthrough_test.go) that exercises this exact pattern and verifies expected behavior.

testing_gaps.title: No regression test demonstrating loss of defaults inheritance
testing_gaps.description: No test asserts that `defaults.failsafe: [{matchMethod:'*', retry:{maxAttempts:3}}]` plus `networks.failsafe: [{matchMethod:'trace_*', timeout:10s}]` results in trace_* having NO retry policy post-PR (the documented behavior change). Add this as a positive test under common/defaults_test.go to lock in the contract and surface it in code review of future config changes.

testing_gaps.title: No race test for matchers.Match against concurrent NormalizeHttpJsonRpc
testing_gaps.description: No `go test -race` test exercises matchers.Match while NormalizeHttpJsonRpc concurrently mutates Params. Add a hedge-scenario test in erpc/networks_hedge_test.go that uses params-based matchers, runs with -race, and verifies no data race is reported.

testing_gaps.title: No telemetry test asserting matcher identity in spans/metrics
testing_gaps.description: No test verifies that span attributes correctly identify which matcher was selected, particularly when the same FailsafeExecutor matches both trace_* and debug_* via a single matcher. Once rel-004 is fixed, add a span-assertion test.

testing_gaps.title: No idempotency test for repeated SetDefaults / Validate cycles
testing_gaps.description: data/cache_policy.go now calls cfg.SetDefaults() in NewCachePolicy (cache_policy.go:36). Idempotency relies on the early-return in convertLegacyMatchers (defaults.go:617-619) and the auto-generated-default short-circuit in validateMatchers (validation.go:32-43). Add a test that calls SetDefaults+Validate twice on the same CachePolicyConfig and asserts the matchers slice length and contents are unchanged after the second call.

testing_gaps.title: No test for runtime config reload preserving (or explicitly resetting) circuit breaker state
testing_gaps.description: Per rr-001/rel-006, CB state is reset on reload. No test pins this behavior either way. Add either (a) a test that asserts CB state IS reset on reload (locking in current behavior), or (b) a follow-up issue tracking persistent CB state.
